narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles like yours from past students. The model looked at key aspects of your academic record, test scores, and planned study approach, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 27, Year 1 school decile of 3, Year 3 decile of 1, and your choice to attend part-time. It predicted a 58% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially, which crossed the threshold for rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 academic decile ranking of 1, well below the typical student's 5.53 average, signaling a significant drop in performance that strongly concerned the model. Next, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 worked in your favor somewhat, as it pulled the prediction slightly toward passing compared to what the model expected, though not enough to offset other issues. Your decision to study part-time, uncommon among the 92.7% of students who go full-time, also nudged the prediction toward failure, as the model associates part-time status with higher risks. These top three elements—Year 3 deciles, undergrad GPA, and enrollment type—dominated the decision, outweighing factors like your LSAT or family income. Compared to typical students, your lower Year 3 standing and part-time choice stood out as bigger red flags than your already below-average LSAT of 27 versus the 36.7 norm. While your profile shows potential in some areas, the model saw a pattern aligned more with first-time bar failures than successes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and exploring full-time options if feasible. Building a stronger narrative around your middle-income resilience and any upward trends could also help in reapplying or appealing to schools with more holistic reviews. With targeted improvements, you can shift future predictions in your favor.
